chapters.title: Is society heading for serious decline?
chapters.text:
Ask Adam Carolla about the state of modern society and he will be blunt. He does not soften his view. He says the country is in serious decline, and he does not apologize for saying so.

The push to keep moving forward has gone too far. Movement for the sake of movement has become the norm. Carolla gives a clear example with smoking. Long ago, smoking was allowed almost everywhere, and it was even encouraged. Then people realized that smoking was terrible for health. Smokers were pushed out of bars and restaurants and into smoking sections. That did not work either, because secondhand smoke turned out to be harmful too. Smokers were pushed outside, and now everyone seems to vape. Now vaping is on shaky ground too.

Carolla calls this the progressive movement, and he thinks it has gone too far. Should the pace slow down? Should some older parts of society and culture be kept? Does everything have to change and be perfectly politically correct? Because of all of this, Carolla argues that the world is simply less free than it used to be.

Some parts of society and culture do not need to be changed. Movement for the sake of movement does not always lead somewhere good.

Modern society is in serious decline.

chapters.title: Do you really need a dog to get through a trip?
chapters.text:
Be honest. Have you ever taken a dog on a plane or in a car for any reason other than moving from one place to another and having to bring the dog along?

One of Carolla's biggest pet peeves is support animals. He has seen it all, dogs, lizards, cats, even miniature ponies. What is supposed to happen with the animal once the destination is reached? Do hotels all take pets? Which hotel would take a miniature pony?

A support animal is any animal a person needs nearby in order to feel supported and able to handle a task. It is the modern version of a security blanket.

A support animal is an animal carried for emotional comfort, not because the person needs the animal for any practical reason.

Carolla tells a story about a time he saw Henry Cavill, better known as Superman, getting off a plane with a dog. The dog was his support animal. Superman needs a support animal?

Carolla is no fan of support animals, and the very idea of one troubles him. If a person cannot get on a plane without a pet, perhaps a deeper issue is worth looking at. A plane cabin is the wrong setting for an animal.

Emotional support animals are different from service animals. Service animals have been trained to do a specific job, like guiding a blind person.

As Adam Carolla has put it, your dog may be carry-on, but your dignity has been checked.

chapters.title: How can the topic of Donald Trump be avoided?
chapters.text:
Talking about modern society and culture without mentioning Donald Trump is nearly impossible, given his role as President of the United States. Even outside the United States, Trump's words and policies have a huge effect.

Donald Trump has reshaped modern society beyond measure, for better or for worse. That call is up to the reader.

Adam Carolla is not a big Donald Trump fan, and he is not the type to wear a MAGA hat. He met Trump before Trump became president, at the Playboy mansion while filming The Celebrity Apprentice.

As Adam Carolla has written, the man whose hand I shook while naked women painted like Miller Lite bottles played in the grotto is now in charge of our nuclear weapons.

Donald Trump stirs up scandals almost without trying, but he is anything but boring. Trump tells people what they want to hear. He plays to his audience, regardless of the truth, and he wins fans by refusing to back down. Even when he says something wrong and fact-checkers correct him, he sidesteps the correction and calls it fake news.

Trump causes scandals easily, and they divide society while also energizing his base.

Trump's comments spark outrage or unity, depending on which side a person stands. This has reshaped modern society in a big way. Like him or not, if Trump speaks, it is worldwide news within seconds.

Donald Trump is the forty-fifth President of the United States.

chapters.title: Stop casting yourself as the victim
chapters.text:
The world is full of threats. Terrorism, racism, inequality, crime, murder. The list keeps going. But has the fear of what might happen grown so large that the threat feels like it is sitting at the door?

Carolla calls this chick think.

General toughness has been slipping for many years, but it may be lower now than ever. People watch crime shows, read real-life stories in magazines, follow the news, and pay close attention to every word. Carolla thinks this hits women harder than men, which is where his phrase chick think comes from. He uses the phrase and gives his wife as an example.

After their children were born, Carolla's wife, Lynette, became so focused on the kids' safety that he struggled to understand it. He believes this is common among women and has spread to men too, an inability to keep things in proportion and see the world clearly. Instead, the mind thinks, that could happen to me. The thought comes while watching a crime show, while watching the news, while listening to a neighbor's story. The odds are tiny, but the worry grows until the person starts to feel like a victim.

That mindset has to get in the way of living well.

So much real-life information is taken in that negative events feel certain. People become victims for no reason.

Twenty percent of the human brain is wired to look for threats. That wiring goes back to the days of cavemen. People are always on the lookout for danger, but most of the time the danger is made up.

As Adam Carolla has said, for every bias a person holds, there is a network ready to feed it.

Because of all this, children grow up feeling like victims before anything has happened. People get hooked on threats that do not exist. Life turns into a long series of what-ifs.

chapters.title: Do organizations actually care, or is it all a game?
chapters.text:
Look at advertisements from twenty years ago next to today's, and the difference is huge. Few things show modern society as clearly as the ads on TV and radio.

Today's ads look nothing like the ads of twenty years ago. They mirror the state of modern society.

Carolla remembers the ads he watched as a child. One was for Pledge, a cleaning product. In the ad, a mother-in-law shows up unannounced and starts checking the house for dust. She shames the wife for not cleaning well enough, then pulls out a can of Pledge and gets to work.

Most ads were made to show women waiting at home for their husband to return from work. Carolla jokes that his wife is more likely to sigh with disappointment when he walks in the door.

Today's ads have left humor and lightness behind. They have been replaced by companies pretending to care. The Subaru ad with the slogan, made with love, is one example. It is a car. It is made of steel.

Carolla argues that society has gone soft, that feelings are being played too much, and that companies use this to make money. Companies also use comparison tactics. A person watches an ad and automatically thinks, I wish I had what that person has. The result is a feeling of being less than.

Organizations are leaning harder on heartstrings as a way to sell products.

Today's ads focus on envy and emotion, a clear mirror of modern society.

chapters.title: Stay out of Twitter wars
chapters.text:
Adam Carolla does not use Facebook, but he is active on Twitter. The part of the platform he dislikes most is the habit of Twitter wars. He finds the term offensive, because it is not really a war. It is one person trying to start a fight, the way children chant fight, fight in a playground.

If someone tries to drag a person into a word fight on social media, what should the person do? Carolla suggests that instead of giving them what they want, like a sarcastic reply or an insult, the person should respond with kindness.

If a negative comment lands on Twitter, the better move is to reply with kindness, not to fire back.

Look at the comment and weigh it. Does the other person have a point? Nobody is always right. Find the places of agreement and disagreement. Be the bigger person and admit fault when it is there. Apologize if a sorry is needed, or leave a kind comment. Nothing frustrates an attacker more, and the person may even learn something.

Not everything on social media has to be a fight or a game. Late-night or early-morning tweets from Donald Trump do not stir up violence any more than some video games, like Grand Theft Auto, do.

Twitter does not have to be a game or a place to fight. It can be a place to learn and share information.

The short version is, never write anything on Twitter that the person would not say to the other person's face.

Around five hundred million tweets are sent every day on average.

chapters.title: The modern habit of being easily offended
chapters.text:
It feels like these days, no move can be made without someone correcting it, in person or, more likely, online. People get upset over the smallest things, and it is hard to take a breath without worrying that the wrong word or action is about to slip out.

Cultural appropriation is a real issue in the modern world. If a person is genuinely committing cultural appropriation, the act is wrong. The trouble is that many people get accused of appropriation when they are doing nothing of the kind.

Cultural appropriation causes real pain and upset, and the issue is worth taking seriously.

Carolla offers two examples to think about. First, two women ran a burrito stand in Portland. The stand was forced down because the women said openly that they had visited Mexico and learned to make real tortillas by peeking through restaurant windows. Is that cultural appropriation, or do they just love burritos?

Second, the president of the University of Louisville had to apologize for wearing a Mexican-themed Halloween costume. Is that cultural appropriation, or just Halloween fun? The reader can decide, but Carolla argues that a Mexican man working hard in the desert, wearing a sombrero to keep the sun off, is unlikely to be offended by that kind of costume.

Has the line moved too far? Has the real meaning of appropriation been lost? Carolla thinks so.

Cultural appropriation has been defined as the unacknowledged or inappropriate adoption of the customs, practices, or ideas of one people or society by members of another, usually more dominant, people or society.

chapters.title: Keep one eye on the facts
chapters.text:
Before turning to the MeToo movement, Adam Carolla wants to be clear. He backs the movement fully, and he is completely against rape of any kind. He feels silly even having to say that, because rape and unwanted advances are obviously wrong. Still, in modern society, a person has to make their stance plain to avoid confusion.

The MeToo movement has been a strong step forward for women's rights and has brought a measure of justice to many women who went through terrible experiences. Carolla argues, though, that finger-pointing without fact or evidence should be avoided. He calls the result collateral damage.

Once the rumor mill starts spinning, rumors of that size can be treated as fact. Once a person is tagged, true or not, the label is hard to shake if the person did nothing wrong.

Rumors can easily turn into fact, so fingers should not be pointed until the case is proven. The accused should be presumed innocent until the evidence is clear.

Because of all this, the rules of dating have changed beyond recognition, especially for men. Stories from parents, especially from when they were courting, would not fly today. A man who kept sending a woman flowers until she said yes would have been seen as romantic decades ago, but today the same behavior is labeled stalking. Carolla says he would hate to be dating in this day and age.

The MeToo movement is a major part of modern society, but some men belong in its story and some do not. A few names belong there, and a few do not.

The hashtag MeToo was posted by Alyssa Milano at noon on October 15, 2017. By the end of the day, it had been used more than two hundred thousand times.

chapters.title: What does the future hold?
chapters.text:
Society and culture have shifted a great deal over the last few decades. Some of the changes lift people up, and many pull them down. Is it too late to turn the tide on the bad parts? Carolla does not think so.

The first move toward taking back control is to stop leaning on convenience. Food shows up at the door, nearly everything is done online, and most people barely leave the sofa. That is not living. Less convenience and more doing, with the hands and the body, is a strong place to start.

As Adam Carolla has put it, convenience is going to convenience the world out of existence.

What else helps? Carolla believes people should step past their comfort zone and move more. Take on a small challenge, cut sugar and grains for a while, and see how the body responds. The slow poisoning of the modern diet is real, and most people do not even see it.

Some things in life do not give instant pleasure, and that is fine. The good feelings do not need to come the moment the action is taken. Push the self a little, and feel more alive as a result. Take a risk and step out the door. That is the only way to fix the lack of energy and focus that fills the modern day. Society has gone soft, and it is time to find itself again.

Too much time is spent chasing convenience and the easy road. Step past the comfort zone and take a few risks. Find the self once more.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
There are many signs pointing to the decline of modern society, but that does not mean the slide cannot be reversed. Some of the changes should stay, because they teach where the line should and should not be.

Society shapes culture, and culture never stays still for long. Change can be good or bad, and most of it is open to interpretation.

I'm Your Emotional Support Animal is full of opinions. Some readers will agree and some will not, but the book gives plenty to think about.

A few small practices can carry these ideas forward. First, take a clear look at modern society as a whole. Which parts feel right, and which parts feel wrong? Second, the next time an angry or inflammatory post shows up on Twitter, refuse to take the bait. Choose kindness instead. Third, do not become a victim for no reason. Just because something happened to one person does not mean it is about to happen to the reader too.
